sentences.text: We heard it, yes.
We--
We-- see, my uncle was a lithographist, had his own color printing.
He even printed false documents for some people who succeeded to get away from Poland.
My uncle's name?
Leiberman.
Yeah.
Sandor.
That's in English, Alex.
Leiberman.
We, we had a number of people from Poland who were given-- everybody had to have a police document that, that you are a citizen.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So these people who came from Galicia and, and other occupied territories of Poland, they were given a false certificate and also were kept in the synagogue, in a large synagogue.
And, and they had other building next to the synagogue.
Because they couldn't speak the language, they were told not to rely on that certificate only, because they, they can be caught.
If someone checks people in the street, and you can't speak the language, doesn't matter if you had that false certificate, which looked, looked real, was like a real.
I remember a few, yes, because I knew, I went in the-- one of the rooms where they feed-- they were at-- they were given food, were brought food, and all.
People from the community.
It was a Jewish community of 15,000 to 20,000, with the area around that Oradea, was over 15,000 Jewish people.
No.
They didn't know about it.
That's right.
No, the shul was about a good kilometer from me, from where we lived, where my uncle lived.
But we knew about them, because they were-- they never had children with them.
They, they took off by themselves, each-- they could run.
They must have come out from some concentration camps, even, because they told us what happened to them, and what happens to other people.
Well, I remember vaguely.
[BACKROUND NOISE] And I remember also some people who lived in Hungary, but their ancestors came from Galicia, were expelled.
They had expulsion orders.
And they couldn't even speak another language, only Hungarian.
But they were expelled and taken to the border, and, and let them go over the border, even a couple of shots in the air to make them run faster.
The other side was already occupied by Germany, so what happened to them--
Yeah, we knew, but see, I was a more or less [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had access to a shortwave radio.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Radios were-- shortwave radios who were held by Jewish people had to be sealed in the back and set to-- only to the Hungarian government station.
So you couldn't have the dial moving.
Only the Jewish people.
Because-- so they couldn't listen in to London or Moscow during the war.
They, they come and check you occasionally.
And then you could get six months' imprisonment for not sealing your radio onto the one station.
So what happens frequently, when I went on the summer holiday home to our village, no one had a radio in- in the village.
There was only one official who had a radio, a Hungarian man.
And that was operating on batteries, car batteries.
And I went to Szilagy-Somlyo that 18 kilometers away, on a pushbike.
Took me about three quarters of an hour to an hour.
And I had to memorize the news.
No, the community in the synagogue, where I had to come back and tell the news.
No.

They could catch me with, with, with the writings, like how many bombs they delivered in various cities in West Germany in the rural, different cities, and where the front line is, because from the local papers, you could only figure out the front line, especially on the Russian front, three weeks-- what, what happened three weeks earlier they announced yesterday.
That went normally.
My, my parents in the village couldn't leave.
They were hard to move.
There's no--
No, they didn't.
They actually-- my uncle had a, a German partner, actually was a volksdeutsch who lived in Hungary.
And he could have his lithography only if he had a non-Jew partner.
So this German was a good partner.
He actually wanted to hide him.
He had wine-- what do you call, grape.
He produced a bit of wine.
He had vines.
He had grapes in the hills.
But they never took up the offer.
No.
My auntie did.
My uncle didn't.
They had regulations.
We finished school and sent home, and...
In March.
Sixth form.
No, the normal year ends approximately on the 20th of June.
And it was approximately the 20th of March, or 20-29 of March, I think, when the Germans came in, end of March.
They sent straightaway home the, the children who were from provinces, not from the city.
And after a week, they closed the whole school.
Yeah.
Yeah.
After that, they order-- they ordered the deportation, the Hungarian government.
Oh, about four weeks, four or five weeks after, sometimes in April.
They read-- two military police appeared, and--
Yeah, they- they appeared, the local military police.
And they read government's rule, that...
Well, that they have to remove us, and, and not to return to this place at all, ever.
So they didn't-- weren't specific.
Just you had to give them any valuables.
Yeah.
And- and-
They- Money and valuables.
And you could take small carriage-- what you can carry with you.
And we were taken to the synagogue next door to us.
And all Jewish families from other villages were there for about two days.

And then we were taken by drawn horse carriages to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the Somlyo-- Szilagy-Somlyo that, that town 18 kilometers away, which they had a disused brick factory, which had a railway line come to it.
The Hungarian name?
Szilagy-Somlyo.
S-Z-I-L-A-G-Y dash Somlyo, S-O-M-L-Y-O.
To that, that's-- actually, they were gendarmes--
--from the village.
Yes, correct.
I, I only took some food.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some clothing.
It was-- we were heading for spring.
It was spring, practically, already.
And I didn't take too many things.
Well, I, I remember one thing.
They-- I had a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What I took, I don't remember.
But what I didn't take, I remember more.
They took my watch, a Certina chrome Swiss watch.
And I hid my bike.
I had a, a bike, a German Miele.
They produced bikes those days.
Now they produce [LAUGHS] dishwashers, mainly.
And I even cut the tires.
The tires of your bike.
Yeah.
Well, she-- they confiscated the jewelry.
We had to give what she had on her.
No, I don't remember.
I don't think she took with her-- they were-- they were actually waiting for you to pack and go.
They stood over you while--
They stood over you, yeah.
It was a matter of half an hour, and off you-- off you go.
You signed some--
Into the synagogue, yeah.
And people from other villages.
And they kept you there for a couple of days.
Two days.
And then they transported you to, to the-- to the brick-- disused brick-
To that brick-- disused brick factory, which was-- they set up as a ghetto.
By horse-drawn carriage.
Open carriages.
Oh, there were about 2,000 people there, from the whole area, from roughly up to about 100 kilometers away.
That was a whole shire, like they had about 70-- the whole country was divided into about 70 districts.
Each had their own judiciary.
Actually, the man in charge for, for, for that ghetto, the Hungarian man, he was the chief justice of this area.
[LAUGHS] And the ghetto had only two German soldiers.
All the rest were Hungarians, gendarmes.
That was close to a river, a small river.
And no people lived there close by.
The town would have been about three, four kilometers from, from the brick factory.
But the brick factory has its own railway tracks.
Just the factory had a fence, but we couldn't be accommodated in the factory.
We were sleeping in the open fields next to the factory.
Actually, it was open fields.
No shelter.
The-- some women had shelter in the-- next to the kiln.
They had storages.
But I was in the open.
Oh, about two weeks.
Up till about mid-April--
--or, or end of April.
Well, that was made into a ghetto when these orders came through.
Yes...Yes, that made it into a ghetto.
Yeah, that was for all this district of-- called Szilagy.
That's the area.
All the Jewish people, wherever they lived in this area, came to this place.
Everybody, yeah.
Yes, I do.
I actually [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] volunteered [LAUGHS] to cut some timber for the-- for the kitchen, which supplied the food for the-- for the guards, the gendarmes.
We got some extra food for cutting the timber.
Yes.
No, they were in a different ghetto in the same town where the school was.
It was a much bigger center.
But when I cut wood, these two people who survived with me-- they're in Israel.
The three of us--
Not school friends, only school friends from primary school.
They didn't come in town.
One is deaf and dumb now.
And I visited him three years ago.
And the other one-- the three of us, we cut wood.
So we saw what's going on there.
They did a lot of-- they try to force all sorts of things out of different people.
They thought they can-- they can get their jewelry.
Some have.
Some didn't even have it where they hid it.
And they were beating them up there.
The Hungarian, Hungarian gendarmes.
Yeah, Hungarian.
And they had all sorts of ways of making them talk.
Well, I, I saw a young woman, probably in her early 20s.
They, they put under her knee a broom stick.
At the back of her knees...
Yes..
In the back of her knees, which went through her two arms cross, like this.
So it went-- her arms had to go--
The arms had to go--
--the knees up, and the stick through here.
And then your hands were tied up with string.
And you actually, if they hit her, she were moving like a ball.
She couldn't stretch, not foot, not, not hands.
And some men were tied with their hands in the back, so the tip of their toe was reaching the ground, all their weight.
And they would pull them up on some live trees.
They hung them from trees by--
From trees.
So they're only 10 meters away.
They're hanging them up there.
And, and the weight of them were on the shoulder.
They could be conscious up to 10 minutes, and then they lost consciousness.
In the back.
And their hands-- and the rope from the hands--
The rope from the hand was--
--on the-- pulled up on the tree until the weight-- all their weight was on, on the tip.
They were not fully in the air.
Tip on the floor.
The tip of their feet touched the ground.
So they passed out usually between 10 and 15 minutes.
And then they let them loose.
And they poured a bucket or two of cold water over their head, so they revived.
And either they let them go, and next day they started again, or they even started-- they put them up straightaway.
No, I worked in the open, next to the kitchen.
I just cut wood there.
And I saw it.
I was close by.
And they were not-- they didn't do it in masses.
They took out one or two at a time, and- and only people which they saw they are rich, or they didn't disclose whatever they had, or, or they had personal--
That's all they wanted.
Hungarian.
See, there were only two German soldiers there.
They were more or less in the background.
But they probably told them what to do, because two Germans couldn't keep over 2,000 people.
They had to have Hungarian guards there.
I had a bit of extra food, plus we had some dry food-- dry food [BACKROUND NOISE] from when we were taken from the synagogue to the ghetto.
We went like a long caravan.
Maybe was about-- were about 30 carriages.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were handed food from peasants from the village.
Yes.
No.
There were only two more villages to be passed through.
But no, we -we got-- I remember I got sausages from-- smoked sausages from Romanian peasants, mainly Romanians.
Yes, we, we, we could keep it or eat it.
Oh yes, we got extra bread.
But in the ghetto, they didn't supply food for the people.
Everybody had to have some dry food to keep them going for two weeks.
There was no, no food, like a kitchen, supplies, anything, even tea or coffee.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Of course, there was no-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was no running water there, just a well and the river.
And of course, they had to build some temporary toilet facilities, which were just along, along the lines of timber, with, with a bit of a fence in between male and female toilets.
It weren't even a hole.
It was a trench.
And no, no cubicles or anything like that.
It was a fence with-- it was a trench with partitions in it, so that people could be separated from one another.
No separation.
It was just a long--
A long trench for men and a long trench for women.
That's right.
They were close to each other.
But they-- one couldn't see the other.
Like, they had a sort of a fence between them.
But each could see each other inside.
Yes, they, they guarded it all, all around the ghetto, Hungarian guards.
I could have escaped if I wanted to.
But I don't think my parents could have.
They..
I could have escaped my own, yes.
Very few.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know anybody who, who escaped from that ghetto.
We were-- they brought train, train carriages.
And they started deporting us.
I was among the first.
There was a few transports going there.
I was, I think, in the second.
The first went, and next day, I went.
With me together.
We went-- they, they were Hungarian guards on cattle trains. , and they had only a small window, a small opening, a good strain, with barbed wire over it.
And the doors were shut.
And they tell a story that you're going to-- you won't-- you won't be taken out of the country, and all stories like that, so they wouldn't cause any panic.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we were taken to a town on the-- Hungarian town which was once Slovakia called Kass-- Kosice-- Kass.
K-A- double S, or the Czech name now is Kosice, K-O-S-I-C-E.
And at, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at the railway station, they-- we could get off the train, one person, if you had any dish, to collect some water, fresh water, drinking water.
And then we only stopped for about 15 minutes.
And then they changed the guards there.
The Hungarians retreated, and the German guards took over, and moved off to, to Birkenau, Auschwitz.
Yes, very full.
Only Hungarian Jews.
The Romanian Jews were not deported.
They, they, they were not on the train.
They were on a separate-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they had guard, little guard cubicles on the train.
They were there.
And they had a carriage-- passenger carriage for the guards only.
For the German guards.
Can you repeat it, please?
Yeah.
No.
That was a full load from, from the--
--brick factory right through.
That's right.
Oh, about [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] about one night and part of two days.
No food.
That's right.
Yeah.
Yeah.
When we arrived there, former prisoners opened the doors, who were working for--
Jewish, in striped uniform.
And everyone had to get off the train, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] lined up five in a row, male separately, and females separately, and go to a selection table.
And a German officer was looking at everybody and having a small stick, about-- could be 18 inches.
And he was waving right or left.
Those who were fit to work, he signaled to go to the right.
And the same system was another officer at another table for the women.
So...
Yeah.
Our own clothes.
My mother was quite young.
She was 42.
And she was sent to the left, because she kept-- [PAUSES FOR 26 SECONDS] [CRYING] she kept the small child of someone else.
She was a good friend-- she was a good friend of hers, district doctor's wife.
And this doctor himself was from Czech territory, Hungarian-attached Czechoslovakia.
And he was doing sort of a military service and appointed to our village, and to seven villages as a-- as a doctor.
The small one could have been, her mother held, about a year and a half.
And she had another one two and a half, and another one three and a half or four, three kids.
And the Germans didn't separate mothers from babies or children.
So even if she would have fitted for work, if she had someone else's child, they would send her to the left.
No.
It was the first you head about it when you actually saw it happen.
Yes.
Yeah, she carried the child.
She carried one.
And her friend, the doctor's wife, carried the other two children.
Of course.
Both, both of them.
And they, they told, told us that in, in the weekend, the families will be reunited.
Well, you believe it or you not.
But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you couldn't do much.
My father was with me.
He was-- he was sent with you.
Yes.
No.
After next day, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I wrote a letter.
I got hold of a small pencil with one of the-- what you call them?
Outside of a tree.
The--
The bark.
There were some bits of white barks.
They were a particular tree that grow in Poland, in Russia, completely white bark.
You could peel it off and write on it like on paper.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wrote a few words.
And I throw it over to a camp which accommodated children who were at the age of 6-- between 6 and 15, girls and boys.
And they were even-- some were even praying with-- with [INAUDIBLE] in the morning.
The Germans kept them.
Whenever a Swiss Red Cross comes to visit, they will show they have a special camp for the kids.
That camp was between, between our camp, which was called zigeunerlager, gypsy camp, because German gypsies were in charge of each barrack.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I ask that kid to throw it over to the next camp, where our women were.
And my auntie, who survived, she got that note from-- she told me after she got liberated, when we met.
Just a few words that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] whatever they told [? her, ?] that we can see them.
